name: LV Hypertrophy
identifyBy: Increased QRS voltage: S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 >35mm (Sokolow-Lyon criteria)
Strain pattern: ST depression and T wave inversion in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5–V6)
ST changes are in direction opposite to QRS ('strain' = LV pressure overload)
Prolonged QRS intrinsicoid deflection time (>50ms in V5–V6)

measurements.stSegment: Depression with T inversion in V5–V6, I, aVL (lateral strain)

management: LVH itself is not treated - it is a marker of underlying disease
Treat the cause: hypertension (most common), aortic stenosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, coarctation of aorta
Antihypertensive therapy: ACEi/ARB shown to reverse LVH over time (most evidence)
Screen for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: echocardiogram is definitive
Stroke risk: LVH with HTN doubles stroke risk - aggressive BP control
Exercise restriction in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with outflow obstruction or VT
pearls: LVH by voltage alone is a sensitive but non-specific finding - confirm with echo
Strain pattern (ST/T changes) increases specificity for pathological LVH
Cornell criteria (R aVL + S V3 >20mm women, >28mm men) less body-habitus dependent than Sokolow-Lyon
Sokolow-Lyon false negatives: obese patients, LBBB, COPD (low voltage masks criteria)
LVH + LBBB: electrolyte and ischaemia changes are masked - echo is essential
differentials: Normal high voltage in thin young patients (no strain pattern)
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (asymmetric hypertrophy, outflow obstruction, family history)
Athlete's heart (high voltage but no strain, physiological bradycardia)
